Scenerio Based Questions

Practical Questions

RAG Question

Answer: This is a classic RAG tuning problem — and the fix isn't just changing K. Here's how I'd approach it:

  • Use metadata filtering first — Before vector search, filter by price (≤ ₹50,000), category (laptops), and other hard constraints. This shrinks the search space to only relevant products.

  • Switch to dynamic K — Instead of a fixed K, retrieve based on a similarity score threshold. Only return products above a certain relevance score, not a fixed count.

  • Re-rank after retrieval — Use a cross-encoder or re-ranker model to re-score the retrieved products against the full query. This pushes the most relevant ones to the top.

  • Query decomposition — Break the query into parts: "lightweight", "college use", "under ₹50,000". Use each part to filter and rank better.

  • Hybrid search — Combine keyword search (for price/specs) with vector search (for semantic meaning like "college use"). This gives you the best of both worlds.

The goal is: retrieve fewer but better results, not more.

LLM cost increased 100x as users grew from 1K to 100K — how to reduce it?

Answer: Scaling from 1K to 100K users without cost exploding is a real engineering challenge. Here's how I'd reduce LLM costs:

  • Cache repeated responses — If many users ask similar questions, cache the LLM output. No need to call the API again for the same query.

  • Use a smaller model for simple tasks — Not every query needs GPT-4. Route simple questions to a cheaper, faster model and only use the big model for complex ones.

  • Reduce prompt size — Trim system prompts, remove unnecessary context, and compress retrieved chunks. Fewer tokens = lower cost.

  • Batch requests where possible — Instead of calling the LLM one request at a time, batch low-priority requests to use cheaper batch APIs.

  • Rate limiting and user quotas — Prevent abuse and unnecessary calls by adding limits per user.

  • Use prompt caching — Some providers (like Anthropic) offer prompt caching for repeated system prompts. This alone can cut costs significantly.

  • Evaluate if LLM is even needed — Some tasks can be handled by simpler rule-based logic or smaller fine-tuned models.

Cost optimization is about being smart — not just scaling infrastructure blindly.

[Image/Media]

We want to build a RAG app using our docs — what can be the monthly estimated cost?

Answer: Great question — and a very real one in interviews! Here's how I'd break it down:

A RAG app has 3 main cost components:

1. Embedding cost (one-time + updates)

  • You convert your docs into vectors once

  • Example: 1 million tokens with OpenAI embeddings ≈ $0.10 (very cheap)

  • Only re-run when docs change

2. Vector database cost (monthly)

  • Pinecone / Weaviate / Qdrant hosting

  • Roughly $0–$70/month depending on size and provider

  • Small doc sets can even use free tiers

3. LLM inference cost (monthly — the big one)

  • Depends on: number of users × queries/day × tokens per query

  • Example: 1,000 users, 10 queries/day, ~2,000 tokens each = 20 million tokens/month = ~$20–$60/month with GPT-3.5 or Claude Haiku

Total ballpark for a small/medium RAG app: $50–$200/month For enterprise scale: $500–$5,000+/month

Always ask leadership: How many users? How many queries per day? That's what drives cost the most.

[Image/Media]

LLM Based Question

This is a classic prompt token waste problem. Here's the issue and how to fix it:

The Problem:

  • 10,000 requests/hour × 2,000 system prompt tokens = 20 million tokens/hour just for the system prompt

  • The actual user query is only 40 tokens — less than 2% of your total cost!

  • You're paying a huge amount for tokens that never change.

The Fix — Use Prompt Caching:

  • Providers like Anthropic and OpenAI support prompt caching

  • The system prompt is processed once and cached

  • Subsequent requests reuse the cached version at a much lower cost (up to 90% cheaper for cached tokens)

  • This alone can reduce your hourly token cost dramatically

Other optimizations:

  • Trim your system prompt — remove anything unnecessary

  • Move static context to the system prompt (so it gets cached) and keep only dynamic parts in the user message

  • Monitor token usage per request with logging tools to catch bloat early

The lesson: always separate what changes (user query) from what doesn't (system prompt), and cache aggressively.

LLM Based Question

This is a great system design question — and the honest answer is: it depends on your use case. Here's how I'd think about it:

Ask these questions first:

  • What is the cost of a wrong answer? (High stakes = use larger model)

  • What does the user expect — instant replies or accurate ones?

  • Can you afford latency, or is speed critical?

Use the smaller (faster) model when:

  • It's a chatbot or search where speed matters more

  • Errors are low-risk (e.g., product recommendations, casual Q&A)

  • You need to handle high traffic at low cost

  • You can add guardrails or fallback logic to catch bad outputs

Use the larger (accurate) model when:

  • The task is high-stakes (medical, legal, financial advice)

  • Accuracy directly impacts user trust or business outcomes

  • Latency is acceptable (e.g., async reports, background tasks)

Best production approach — use both with a router:

  • Route simple queries → small model

  • Route complex or high-risk queries → large model

  • This gives you speed + accuracy where each matters most

Don't pick one blindly. Build smart routing based on query complexity.

Vector Database question

Vector DB costs creep up fast as your data grows. Here's how I'd tackle it:

1. Reduce the number of vectors stored

  • Delete duplicate or outdated documents — don't store what you don't need

  • Chunk smarter — larger, meaningful chunks = fewer vectors for the same content

2. Use lower-dimensional embeddings

  • Switching from 1536-dim to 768-dim embeddings cuts storage and query cost nearly in half

  • Use models like text-embedding-3-small (OpenAI) which offer dimension reduction

3. Use quantization

  • Convert float32 vectors to int8 — reduces memory usage by ~4x with minimal accuracy loss

  • Most modern vector DBs (Qdrant, Weaviate) support this natively

4. Tiered storage

  • Keep frequently accessed vectors in fast (expensive) storage

  • Move rarely accessed vectors to cold/cheap storage

5. Choose the right vector DB for your scale

  • For smaller workloads, use open-source self-hosted options (Qdrant, Chroma, Milvus)

  • Avoid over-provisioned managed cloud plans if your usage doesn't justify it

6. Cache frequent queries

  • If the same queries repeat, cache the vector search results — skip the DB call entirely

Cost optimization in vector DBs is mostly about storing less and querying smarter.

RAG based question

Normal caching won't work here — "What is RAG?" and "Can you explain RAG?" are different strings but mean the same thing. Here's how to handle it:

The Solution: Semantic Caching

  • Embed the user query — Convert the incoming query into a vector (embedding)

  • Search the cache using vector similarity — Compare it against previously cached query embeddings

  • Set a similarity threshold — If the similarity score is above, say, 0.92, treat it as a cache hit and return the stored answer

  • If no match found — Run the full RAG pipeline, then store both the query embedding and the response in the cache for next time

Tools you can use:

  • Redis with vector search support

  • GPTCache (purpose-built for this)

  • Qdrant or Pinecone as your semantic cache store

What to cache:

  • The query embedding

  • The final LLM response

  • Optional: the retrieved chunks (for faster re-retrieval)

Important: Set a TTL (time-to-live) on cached responses so stale answers don't stick around forever.

Semantic caching can reduce LLM calls by 30–60% in high-traffic apps — and users get faster responses too.

MCP Based question

Great question — they both connect systems, but they're built for very different purposes.

REST API:

  • A general-purpose way for any two systems to talk over HTTP

  • You define endpoints, call them, get a response

  • Works great for structured, predictable operations (get user, create order, etc.)

  • Stateless — each request is independent

  • You write custom integration code for every tool

MCP (Model Context Protocol):

  • Specifically designed for AI agents and LLMs to interact with tools and data sources

  • Provides a standard interface so an LLM can discover and use tools without custom code per tool

  • Supports stateful, multi-step interactions — the agent can call tools in sequence

  • The LLM understands what tools are available and decides when and how to call them

  • Think of it as a "universal plug" for AI agents

Simple analogy:

  • REST = a phone call where you dial a specific number every time

  • MCP = a smart assistant that knows all your contacts and decides who to call based on what you need

When to use which:

  • Use REST when building traditional APIs for human-facing apps

  • Use MCP when building AI agents that need to dynamically discover and use multiple tools

MCP is quickly becoming the standard for agentic AI systems.

RAG Based Question

30 seconds of silence = users leaving. The fix is streaming — show output as it's being generated, not after.

How Streaming Works: Instead of waiting for the full response, the LLM sends tokens one by one as they're generated. The frontend displays them in real time — just like ChatGPT does.

Here's how to design it end-to-end:

Step 1 — Enable streaming on the LLM call

  • Most APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) support stream=True

  • This returns a stream of tokens instead of one big response

Step 2 — Use Server-Sent Events (SSE) or WebSockets on the backend

  • As tokens arrive from the LLM, push them immediately to the frontend

  • SSE is simpler and works great for one-way streaming (server → client)

Step 3 — Run retrieval before streaming starts

  • Fetch the relevant documents first (retrieval step)

  • Once retrieval is done, start the LLM call with streaming enabled

  • This way, the user sees text appearing within 1–2 seconds

Step 4 — Show progress indicators for slow steps

  • While retrieval is happening, show "Searching documents…" to the user

  • Once streaming starts, replace it with the live token output

Bonus tip: You can even stream partial results from retrieval — show source documents as they're found, then stream the answer on top.

Result: Users feel the system is fast and responsive, even if the full answer takes 30 seconds.

RAG Based Question

This is one of the trickiest RAG problems — retrieval is working, but the LLM is still making things up. Here's why it happens and how to fix it:

Why hallucination still happens even with correct docs:

  • The LLM ignores parts of the retrieved context and fills gaps with its own "knowledge"

  • The retrieved chunks are correct but too long, loosely structured, or poorly formatted

  • The prompt doesn't clearly instruct the model to only use the provided context

  • The LLM tries to sound confident even when the answer isn't in the docs

Fixes:

  • Strengthen the system prompt — Explicitly tell the LLM: "Answer ONLY using the provided context. If the answer is not in the context, say 'I don't know'."

  • Improve chunk quality — Make sure retrieved chunks are clean, focused, and not cut off mid-sentence. Better chunks = less guessing by the LLM.

  • Add a grounding check — After generating the answer, run a second LLM call to verify: "Is this answer fully supported by the context?" If not, flag or regenerate it.

  • Use citation enforcement — Ask the LLM to cite which part of the document supports each claim. This forces it to stay grounded.

  • Reduce context noise — Pass only the top 2–3 most relevant chunks, not 10. More context can sometimes confuse the model.

  • Use smaller, focused prompts — Large, messy prompts increase hallucination risk.

Retrieval is only half the battle. The prompt design and output validation are what truly prevent hallucinations.

LLM Based Question

This is LLM non-determinism — and it's completely normal by default. Here's the full picture:

Why does this happen?

  • Temperature > 0 — The model randomly samples from possible next tokens. Higher temperature = more randomness = different answers each time

  • Top-p / Top-k sampling — These also introduce randomness in how the next token is chosen

  • Hardware-level floating point differences — Even tiny numerical differences across GPU runs can change outputs

  • Silent model updates — If the provider updates the model in the background, outputs can shift

  • Unstable context — If session history, timestamps, or RAG results differ slightly between calls, the output changes too

How to control it in production:

  • Set temperature to 0 — This makes the model pick the highest probability token every time, giving consistent outputs

  • Fix all sampling parameters — Set top_p, top_k, and frequency_penalty explicitly — don't leave them as defaults

  • Use a seed value — Some providers (OpenAI, etc.) support a seed parameter for reproducibility

  • Pin your model version — Don't use gpt-4 (which auto-updates). Use gpt-4-0613 or a specific versioned endpoint

  • Standardize your system prompt — Even small prompt changes cause different outputs

  • Cache responses — For identical queries where consistency is critical, return the cached answer directly

Full determinism is hard to guarantee, but you can get very close with these steps.

LLM Based Question

10 seconds is way too long for most users. Here's a structured approach to diagnose and fix it:

Step 1 — Find where the time is being spent Is it slow retrieval? Slow LLM inference? Network latency? Measure each step before optimizing.

Step 2 — Reduce token count

  • Shorter prompts = faster responses

  • Trim your system prompt, reduce retrieved context size

  • Fewer output tokens also means faster responses — set max_tokens to only what you need

Step 3 — Use a faster / smaller model

  • Switch to a lighter model for simpler queries (e.g., GPT-3.5, Claude Haiku, Gemini Flash)

  • Reserve large models only for complex tasks

Step 4 — Enable streaming

  • The model still takes the same time to generate, but users see output immediately

  • Perceived latency drops dramatically even if total time is the same

Step 5 — Use semantic caching

  • Cache responses for common or repeated queries

  • Return cached answers instantly — no LLM call needed

Step 6 — Optimize your RAG pipeline

  • Run retrieval and any pre-processing in parallel where possible

  • Use faster embedding models for retrieval

Step 7 — Check your infrastructure

  • Are you hitting rate limits? Causing queuing?

  • Is your server in a region far from the API endpoint?

  • Consider dedicated API tiers for lower latency

Latency is a UX problem — always start by measuring, then fix the biggest bottleneck first.

LLM Agent Question

This is a classic "over-calling" or agent loop problem — and it's a real production concern. Here's how to fix it:

Why does this happen?

  • The agent doesn't have a clear stopping condition

  • The prompt doesn't tell it when "enough information" has been gathered

  • The model keeps trying to verify or improve its answer unnecessarily

How to fix it — design a proper stopping mechanism:

  • Add an explicit "I have enough information" action — Give the agent a special tool called something like final_answer(). Instruct it: "Once you have enough information to answer, call final_answer() instead of more tools."

  • Set a max tool call limit — Hard cap the number of tool calls per query (e.g., max 5). If reached, force the agent to respond with what it has.

  • Add a confidence/sufficiency check in the prompt — After each tool result, the agent should ask itself: "Do I have enough to answer the user's question? If yes, stop." Build this logic directly into the system prompt.

  • Use structured output with a "done" flag — Ask the agent to return a JSON with { "is_done": true/false, "answer": "..." }. If is_done is true, stop the loop.

  • Add a loop controller in your orchestration layer — Don't rely only on the LLM to decide. Your code should evaluate whether the agent's last response contains a final answer, and break the loop if so.

  • Log and monitor tool call patterns — Track how many tool calls agents are making per query in production. Sudden spikes = something's wrong.

Agents need guardrails — they won't stop on their own unless you explicitly design them to.

Prompt Engineering

Writing prompts one by one for hundreds of tasks is not scalable. Here's how to approach it systematically:

Step 1 — Group tasks by type

  • Don't treat every task as unique. Categorize them: summarization, classification, extraction, Q&A, generation, etc.

  • Tasks in the same category often need the same prompt structure — just with different inputs

Step 2 — Build prompt templates

  • Create a reusable base template for each category

  • Example template: "You are a [role]. Your job is to [task]. Here is the input: {input}. Output format: [format]."

  • Swap out only the variable parts per task

Step 3 — Use a prompt registry

  • Store all prompts in a centralized place (a database, config file, or prompt management tool like LangSmith or PromptLayer)

  • Name and version each prompt so you can track changes and revert if needed

Step 4 — Test with a prompt evaluation framework

  • Don't just write prompts — test them against sample inputs

  • Score outputs for accuracy, format compliance, and edge cases

  • Automate this with an eval pipeline so you can batch-test all prompts at once

Step 5 — Iterate and improve

  • Track which prompts underperform in production

  • Use logs and user feedback to refine them over time

The goal is to treat prompts like code — structured, versioned, tested, and reusable.

LLM and Tools

This is a deep system design question — and the answer almost never lies in the prompt alone. Here's where to look:

1. Memory and context management

  • Does the better agent maintain relevant conversation history more effectively?

  • Too much context = noise. Too little = the agent forgets critical info.

  • The better agent likely has smarter context pruning or summarization

2. Tool selection and ordering

  • Same tools doesn't mean same tool usage strategy

  • The better agent may be calling tools in a smarter order — gathering the right info before making decisions

  • It might also be skipping unnecessary tool calls (more efficient reasoning)

3. Task decomposition

  • Is one agent breaking complex tasks into smaller sub-tasks first?

  • Agents that plan before acting consistently outperform those that act immediately

4. Error handling and retry logic

  • When a tool fails or returns unexpected output, how does each agent respond?

  • The better agent likely has graceful fallback behavior built in

5. Output parsing and validation

  • One agent might be validating tool outputs before using them

  • Bad intermediate data = bad final answers

6. Feedback loops

  • Is the better agent re-evaluating its own outputs and self-correcting?

  • A simple "reflection step" in the agent loop can dramatically improve quality

7. Prompt structure at the reasoning step

  • Even if the top-level prompt looks the same, how each agent structures its internal reasoning (chain-of-thought) can differ significantly

Same model + same tools ≠ same performance. Agent design is the differentiator.

RAG Similarity Search

This is a classic RAG failure — vector search is great for meaning, but terrible for exact identifiers like order numbers, IDs, and codes. Here's the fix:

The Root Cause: Vector/semantic search finds "similar meaning." But "Order #3766" and "Order #3767" are semantically nearly identical — they differ by just one digit. The embedding model can't tell them apart because it doesn't care about exact character-level differences.

The Fix: Use Hybrid Search

  • Keyword / exact match search — Use BM25 or a traditional full-text search index (like Elasticsearch or PostgreSQL full-text search) alongside your vector search

  • Keyword search is great at finding exact strings like order numbers, IDs, dates, and codes

  • Vector search handles natural language meaning

  • Combine both scores — Use a weighted hybrid: final_score = α × keyword_score + (1-α) × vector_score

  • For queries that look like IDs or numbers, increase the keyword weight automatically

Additional fixes:

  • Metadata filtering — Store order numbers as structured metadata fields, not just embedded text. Filter by order_id = "3766" before doing any semantic search

  • Query type detection — Detect if the user query contains an ID pattern (like #XXXX) and route it to exact search instead of vector search

  • Re-ranker — After retrieval, re-rank results with a model that can catch exact matches and boost them to the top

Rule of thumb: Never rely on vector search alone when your data contains exact identifiers.

LLM Parameters

Great foundational question! Top-p (also called nucleus sampling) controls how creative or focused the model's responses are.

Here's how it works — simply:

When an LLM generates the next word, it has a list of possible words with probability scores. Top-p tells the model: "Only consider words whose combined probability adds up to p."

Example:

  • Top-p = 1.0 → Consider ALL possible words (most random, most creative)

  • Top-p = 0.9 → Only consider the top words that together account for 90% of probability

  • Top-p = 0.1 → Only consider the very highest probability words (most focused, least creative)

In simple terms:

  • High Top-p (close to 1.0) = More variety, more creative, but can go off-track

  • Low Top-p (like 0.1–0.3) = More predictable, consistent, safer outputs

When to use what:

Use Case

Recommended Top-p

Creative writing, brainstorming

0.9 – 1.0

General Q&A chatbots

0.7 – 0.9

Factual answers, code generation

0.1 – 0.5

Critical outputs (medical, legal)

0.1 or lower

Top-p vs Temperature: Both control randomness, but differently:

  • Temperature scales ALL probabilities up or down

  • Top-p cuts off unlikely options entirely

In production, you typically tune both together. A common safe default: temperature=0.7, top_p=0.9.

RAG Vs Fine Tuning

This is one of the most common AI architecture decisions — and knowing when to use each can make or break your product.

Choose RAG when:

  • Your data changes frequently — RAG pulls from a live knowledge base. No retraining needed when docs are updated. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into the model — outdating it quickly.

  • You need source citations — RAG can show exactly which document the answer came from. Fine-tuning can't do this.

  • Your data is large and varied — A 10,000-page document library is much easier to handle with RAG than fitting it all into fine-tuning data.

  • You have a limited budget — RAG only needs an embedding model + vector DB. Fine-tuning requires GPU compute, data preparation, and repeated training runs.

  • You need quick setup — RAG can be production-ready in days. Fine-tuning takes weeks of data prep, training, and evaluation.

Choose Fine-Tuning when:

  • You need a specific tone or style — Teaching a model to always respond in your brand's voice works better with fine-tuning

  • You have a narrow, stable task — Like always extracting structured fields from invoices in a fixed format

  • Latency is critical — Fine-tuned models don't need a retrieval step, so they can be faster

  • You want the model to learn new reasoning patterns, not just new facts

The honest answer in most interviews: Start with RAG. It's faster, cheaper, and easier to maintain. Only consider fine-tuning if RAG clearly can't solve your problem.

LLM API

This is a very real production problem. "Works on my machine" is painful when it hits at scale. Here's exactly what I'd investigate:

1. Concurrent request volume

  • Locally you're sending 1–2 requests at a time. In production, hundreds of users may hit the API simultaneously

  • Check: Are you hitting RPM (requests per minute) or TPM (tokens per minute) limits?

  • Fix: Add a request queue and throttle concurrent calls

2. Shared API key across services

  • If multiple services or workers share the same API key, their usage adds up fast

  • Fix: Use separate API keys per service or environment, or implement a central rate-limit-aware API gateway

3. No retry logic with backoff

  • Locally you retry manually. In production, failed requests just crash

  • Fix: Implement exponential backoff — wait 1s, then 2s, then 4s before retrying on 429 errors

4. Token usage per request

  • Long prompts with large context windows burn through your TPM limit quickly

  • Fix: Log token usage per request, trim prompts, and set max_tokens limits

5. Tier limits vs. actual usage

  • Your free or low-tier API plan may have strict limits that weren't a problem in low-volume local testing

  • Fix: Upgrade your API tier or request a limit increase from OpenAI

6. No rate limit monitoring

  • You won't know you're hitting limits until users complain

  • Fix: Monitor 429 error rates in your logging system (Datadog, Grafana, CloudWatch)

7. Burst traffic patterns

  • All users hitting the API at the same time (e.g., after a marketing push)

  • Fix: Implement a job queue (like Celery or BullMQ) to smooth out traffic spikes

Always design for rate limits from day one — they will hit you in production.

Scenario – 1: Retrieval Pipeline Failure

You built a RAG chatbot in plain Python (chunking + embeddings + FAISS). Users say answers are wrong even though the information exists in the PDF. How would you debug chunking strategy, embedding quality, retrieval scoring, and final prompt design?


Answer:


1) First confirm the failure mode (don’t guess)

Before changing anything, I’d reproduce a “known wrong answer” and log the whole pipeline:

  • What question was asked?

  • What chunks were retrieved (top-k)?

  • What scores/distances did FAISS return?

  • Did the best chunk actually contain the answer?

  • Did the LLM ignore it (prompt issue) or did retrieval fail (chunking/embeddings)?

Minimal debugging logger (must-have)

def debug_rag(query, retriever, llm, top_k=5):
    results = retriever.search(query, top_k=top_k)  # [(chunk, score, meta), ...]

    print("\\n--- QUERY ---")
    print(query)

    print("\\n--- TOP CHUNKS ---")
    for i, (chunk, score, meta) in enumerate(results, 1):
        print(f"\\n#{i} score={score} source={meta.get('source')} page={meta.get('page')}")
        print(chunk[:800])  # preview

    context = "\\n\\n".join([f"[{i}] {c}" for i, (c, _, _) in enumerate(results, 1)])
    answer = llm.generate(query=query, context=context)

    print("\\n--- ANSWER ---")
    print(answer)
    return answer

This tells me instantly: retrieval problem (good chunk not in top-k) vs generation problem (chunk present but ignored).


2) Debug chunking strategy (most common root cause)

What usually goes wrong

  • Chunks too large → embedding becomes “blurry” (many topics mixed).

  • Chunks too small → answer spans multiple chunks; retrieval misses needed pieces.

  • No overlap → splits cut sentences / definitions.

  • Chunk boundaries ignore structure (headings/tables/code).

  • PDF text extraction weird: broken lines, hyphenated words, wrong order.

How I’d test chunking quickly

  1. Pick a question that is failing.

  2. Manually find the answer in the raw extracted text.

  3. Check which chunk contains it.

  4. Ensure the chunk is retrievable.

Better baseline chunking (simple + effective)

  • Use recursive chunking: prefer splitting by \\n\\n (paragraph), then \\n, then . if needed.

  • Typical settings:

    • chunk_size: 600–1200 characters (or 200–400 tokens)

    • overlap: 80–150 characters (or 40–80 tokens)

def chunk_text(text, chunk_size=1000, overlap=150):
    text = " ".join(text.split())  # normalize whitespace
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(text):
        end = min(start + chunk_size, len(text))
        chunk = text[start:end]
        chunks.append(chunk)
        start = end - overlap
        if start < 0:
            start = 0
        if start >= len(text):
            break
    return chunks

Chunk metadata is critical

Store page, section heading, chunk_id. Without this, debugging is painful and citations become impossible.


3) Debug embedding quality (silent killer)

Even with “correct” chunking, embeddings can fail due to:

  • Using a different embedding model during indexing vs querying

  • Wrong preprocessing (e.g., lowercasing everything, removing punctuation, stripping numbers)

  • Bad extracted text (garbage characters)

  • Domain mismatch: PDF is heavy on tables, codes, abbreviations

Checks I’d do immediately

  • Assert embedding dimension is consistent

  • Assert the same model name/version used for both index and query

  • Sanity check similarity on known pairs:

    • Query: “What is the refund policy?”

    • Chunk that contains “refund policy” should be closer than random chunk.

import numpy as np

def cosine_sim(a, b):
    a = a / (np.linalg.norm(a) + 1e-9)
    b = b / (np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-9)
    return float(np.dot(a, b))

def embedding_sanity_check(embed, query, positive_text, negative_text):
    q = embed(query)
    pos = embed(positive_text)
    neg = embed(negative_text)

    print("cos(query, positive) =", cosine_sim(q, pos))
    print("cos(query, negative) =", cosine_sim(q, neg))

If positive isn’t clearly higher than negative, embedding or text quality is suspect.

Fixes if embeddings are weak

  • Improve cleaning: fix hyphenations, remove repeated headers/footers, keep numbers/units.

  • Add “title/heading prefix” into chunk text before embedding:

    • Chunk text = "Section: Pricing\\n\\n" + chunk

      This helps embeddings a lot.


4) Debug retrieval scoring (FAISS pitfalls)

Common mistakes with FAISS

  • Using L2 distance vs cosine similarity incorrectly

  • Forgetting to normalize vectors when using cosine-like behavior

  • Too small top_k (answer exists but is rank 7)

  • Not using MMR / diversity → top chunks are near-duplicates and miss complementary info

  • Using only dense retrieval; some queries need keyword match

Correct approach: normalize + inner product

If you want cosine similarity with FAISS:

  • normalize vectors to unit length

  • use IndexFlatIP (inner product)

import faiss
import numpy as np

def build_faiss_ip_index(vectors):
    vectors = vectors.astype("float32")
    faiss.normalize_L2(vectors)          # crucial for cosine-style
    index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(vectors.shape[1])
    index.add(vectors)
    return index

def faiss_search(index, query_vec, top_k=5):
    q = query_vec.astype("float32").reshape(1, -1)
    faiss.normalize_L2(q)
    scores, idxs = index.search(q, top_k)
    return scores[0], idxs[0]

Add a retrieval “inspection mode”

Return:

  • score

  • chunk text

  • chunk metadata

    So you can see if ranking makes sense.

Consider hybrid retrieval (dense + keyword)

Beginner-friendly but powerful fix:

  • use dense embeddings to get top 30

  • rerank those with simple keyword overlap or BM25-style scoring

    This often solves “answer exists but embedding didn’t catch it.”


5) Debug the final prompt (generation issues)

If retrieved chunks contain the answer but the bot still replies wrong:

that’s almost always prompt + constraints.

Prompt problems

  • No instruction to only use context

  • Context is too long; model “forgets” key lines

  • Not asking model to quote or cite which chunk it used

  • No “I don’t know” behavior; model hallucinates confidently

A strong plain prompt (simple + effective)

You are a helpful assistant. Answer ONLY using the provided context.
If the answer is not in the context, say: "I don't know based on the document."

Context:
{context}

Question:
{question}

Rules:
- Use short, direct answer first.
- Then add "Evidence:" and copy 1-2 exact lines from the context that support the answer.

This forces grounding and makes debugging easy because you see “Evidence”.

Add “answerability check” (very practical)

Before final answering, ask the LLM:

  • “Does the context contain enough information to answer? yes/no”

    If “no”, increase top_k or change query rewriting.


6) Practical debugging checklist I’d say in an interview

  1. Reproduce with a failing question and log: retrieved chunks + scores + final prompt.

  2. If answer not in retrieved chunks:

    • fix chunking (size/overlap/structure)

    • fix embeddings (same model, clean text, headings)

    • fix FAISS scoring (normalize, cosine vs L2, top_k)

    • add hybrid retrieval / reranking

  3. If answer is in retrieved chunks:

    • improve prompt: “answer only from context”, “evidence lines”

    • reduce context noise (deduplicate, MMR)

    • add answerability check + “I don’t know”

  4. Add evaluation:

    • create 20–50 Q/A pairs from the PDF

    • measure retrieval hit rate: “is the gold chunk in top_k?”


7) One metric that makes debugging objective

Retrieval Hit Rate

For known questions, how often does the chunk containing the answer appear in top_k?

If hit rate is low → retrieval problem.

If hit rate is high but answers still wrong → prompt/generation problem.



[Image/Media]

Scenario – 2: Non-Deterministic Model Behavior

Your LLM returns different outputs for the same input in production. What causes this behavior, and how would you make the system deterministic?



Answer:

In production, “same input → different output” is normal for LLMs unless you deliberately lock things down. The variance usually comes from sampling randomness, context differences, or infrastructure differences.

What causes different outputs?

1) Sampling randomness (most common)

LLMs often generate using probabilistic sampling:

  • temperature > 0 (adds randomness)

  • top_p / top_k (nucleus/top-k sampling)

  • presence/frequency penalties (push model away from repeating tokens)

    Even tiny randomness early in generation causes totally different later text.

2) Hidden input differences (prompt is not actually the same)

In prod, the “input” is more than the user text. Output can change if any of these differ:

  • System prompt or developer prompt changed

  • Retrieved RAG context changed (different chunks, different ordering)

  • Conversation history differs (even one extra space/turn)

  • Tool outputs (web/RAG results) vary

  • Different formatting, whitespace, timestamps, locale, user profile data

3) Model / serving differences

  • Different model version (even minor revision)

  • Different endpoint settings (default temperature differs)

  • Different tokenization due to SDK/version changes

  • Load balancing across deployments with slightly different configs

4) Concurrency / streaming / truncation effects

  • Streaming can mask partial outputs

  • Token limits cause truncation differently depending on context size

  • Retries: if the first call times out and you retry, you might get a different sample

How to make it deterministic (as much as possible)

Step 1: Set deterministic decoding

  • Set temperature = 0

  • Set top_p = 1 (or disable sampling)

  • Set penalties to 0 (unless you truly need them)

  • Keep max_tokens fixed

This makes the model use greedy/near-greedy decoding, which is the biggest lever.

Step 2: Make the full prompt identical every time

To do this in production:

  • Freeze the system prompt (version it like system_prompt_v7)

  • Ensure the same conversation history is passed (no hidden extra messages)

  • Normalize user input (trim weird whitespace, normalize line endings)

  • If using RAG, ensure retrieval is stable (see next step)

Step 3: Make retrieval deterministic (RAG systems)

RAG is a major source of “same question, different context”:

  • Always use the same embedding model + same preprocessing

  • Fix top_k

  • Sort retrieved chunks consistently:

    • primary: similarity score desc

    • tie-breaker: chunk_id asc

  • Use deterministic chunking and stable document IDs

  • If approximate FAISS index (IVF/HNSW), results can vary slightly; for maximum determinism:

    • use exact search index when feasible (e.g., IndexFlatIP)

    • or fix HNSW params and accept small variance

Step 4: Fix infra behaviors that cause drift

  • Disable or control retries (or log “retry happened”)

  • Pin model version explicitly (don’t use floating “latest”)

  • Keep token limits constant and avoid dynamic prompt additions (timestamps, random IDs)

Step 5: Add output constraints for stability

Even with deterministic decoding, answers can vary if the prompt allows many valid formats. Tighten it:

  • Require JSON schema / strict template

  • Provide examples (few-shot) of the exact format you want

  • Ask for shorter, structured outputs

A practical “deterministic mode” checklist (what I’d say in an interview)

  1. Set temperature=0, top_p=1, penalties = 0.

  2. Pin the exact model version.

  3. Log and hash the final assembled prompt (system + user + retrieved context).

  4. Make RAG stable: same chunking, same embedding model, stable sort, fixed top_k.

  5. Remove non-deterministic inputs (timestamps, randomized ordering, tool variability).

  6. Add structured output format (JSON / template) to reduce degrees of freedom.

Minimal Python-style config example (generic)

LLM_CONFIG = {
    "model": "PINNED_MODEL_VERSION",
    "temperature": 0.0,
    "top_p": 1.0,
    "presence_penalty": 0.0,
    "frequency_penalty": 0.0,
    "max_tokens": 400,
}

def stable_sort_results(results):
    # results: list of dicts {score, chunk_id, text}
    return sorted(results, key=lambda r: (-r["score"], r["chunk_id"]))

One important truth to mention in interviews:

Even with all of the above, perfect determinism isn’t guaranteed in every hosted setup (due to backend changes), but you can get it practically deterministic by locking decoding + model + prompt + retrieval.

[Image/Media]

Scenario – 3: Structured Output Integrity Breakdown

Your “JSON-only” response still includes extra text. How would you enforce strict JSON formatting and implement safe retry logic?



Answer:

To enforce strict JSON-only, you need two things:

  1. Hard constraints (model-side): use structured outputs / JSON schema mode if your LLM supports it.

  2. Guardrails (app-side): validate → if invalid, run a safe, bounded retry that only asks to “fix JSON” (no new content).

Below is an interview-ready answer + a plain-Python implementation.

Why “JSON-only” still leaks extra text

Even if you say “return JSON only,” the model may add:

  • “Sure, here’s the JSON:”

  • Markdown fences ```json

  • trailing explanations

  • malformed JSON (single quotes, trailing commas)

So you must validate programmatically.

How to enforce strict JSON formatting

A) Best option: Structured Outputs / JSON Schema mode

If your provider supports “response_format: json_schema” (or equivalent), use it. That makes the model emit valid JSON that matches a schema (or it fails).

If you can’t rely on that, do B.

B) App-side enforcement (works everywhere)

  1. Prompt with a strict contract (and consequences).

  2. Parse + validate the response as JSON (and schema-validate if needed).

  3. If invalid: run a repair retry that:

    • includes the invalid output

    • says: “Return only corrected JSON, no other text”

    • does not allow adding new keys/fields unless your rules permit it

  4. Bounded retries (e.g., max 2). If still failing, return an error.

Prompt pattern that reduces JSON leaks

Use something like:

  • “Output must be a single JSON object. No markdown. No code fences. No surrounding text.”

  • “If you cannot comply, output {}.”

Also: keep the output schema small and explicit.

Safe retry logic (plain Python)

1) JSON parsing helper (strict)

This tries:

  • direct json.loads

  • if that fails, tries to extract the first { ... } block safely

  • still fails → invalid

import json
import re
from typing import Any, Dict, Tuple, Optional

def try_parse_json(text: str) -> Tuple[bool, Optional[Dict[str, Any]], str]:
    """
    Returns (ok, parsed_json, error_message).
    Strict: must be a JSON object (dict).
    """
    raw = text.strip()

    # Fast path
    try:
        parsed = json.loads(raw)
        if isinstance(parsed, dict):
            return True, parsed, ""
        return False, None, "Parsed JSON is not an object (dict)."
    except Exception as e1:
        pass

    # Recovery attempt: extract first {...} block (common when model adds extra text)
    match = re.search(r"\\{.*\\}", raw, flags=re.DOTALL)
    if match:
        candidate = match.group(0).strip()
        try:
            parsed = json.loads(candidate)
            if isinstance(parsed, dict):
                return True, parsed, ""
            return False, None, "Extracted JSON is not an object (dict)."
        except Exception as e2:
            return False, None, f"JSON parse failed after extraction: {e2}"

    return False, None, "No JSON object found."

2) Minimal schema validation (optional but recommended)

If you know required keys, validate them.

def validate_schema(obj: Dict[str, Any], required_keys: list[str]) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
    missing = [k for k in required_keys if k not in obj]
    if missing:
        return False, f"Missing required keys: {missing}"
    return True, ""

3) Safe retry wrapper

Key safety rules:

  • max retries

  • retry prompt only asks to fix formatting, not regenerate content

  • on retry, we pass the model output and ask it to return corrected JSON only

  • we keep the original user request so the model doesn’t “lose” intent, but we emphasize “no new info; only reformat”

def call_llm(messages: list[dict], config: dict) -> str:
    """
    Placeholder: integrate your LLM call here.
    Must return the raw text response.
    """
    raise NotImplementedError

def get_strict_json(
    user_prompt: str,
    required_keys: list[str] | None = None,
    max_attempts: int = 3,
) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Attempts:
      1) Normal generation with strict JSON instruction
      2..N) Repair attempts: "Fix this into valid JSON ONLY"
    """
    if required_keys is None:
        required_keys = []

    base_system = (
        "You are a service that outputs ONLY valid JSON.\\n"
        "Rules:\\n"
        "- Output must be a single JSON object.\\n"
        "- No extra text, no markdown, no code fences.\\n"
        "- Use double quotes for all strings.\\n"
        "- Do not include trailing commas.\\n"
    )

    # Attempt 1: ask for JSON directly
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": base_system},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt},
    ]

    last_error = ""
    last_raw = ""

    for attempt in range(1, max_attempts + 1):
        raw = call_llm(messages, config={"temperature": 0.0, "top_p": 1.0})
        last_raw = raw

        ok, parsed, err = try_parse_json(raw)
        if not ok:
            last_error = err
        else:
            # Optional schema checks
            if required_keys:
                schema_ok, schema_err = validate_schema(parsed, required_keys)
                if schema_ok:
                    return parsed
                last_error = schema_err

        # If we are here, it failed. Prepare safe repair retry.
        if attempt < max_attempts:
            messages = [
                {"role": "system", "content": base_system},
                {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt},
                {
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": (
                        "The previous output was invalid.\\n"
                        "Return ONLY a corrected JSON object that matches the intended schema.\\n"
                        "Do NOT add any explanation text.\\n"
                        "Do NOT wrap in markdown.\\n"
                        "If keys are missing, include them with null.\\n\\n"
                        "INVALID OUTPUT:\\n"
                        f"{last_raw}"
                    ),
                },
            ]

    # Final fail-safe: raise with debug info (or return a controlled error JSON)
    raise ValueError(
        f"Failed to produce strict JSON after {max_attempts} attempts. "
        f"Last error: {last_error}. Last raw: {last_raw[:500]}"
    )

Why this retry is “safe”:

  • It’s bounded (no infinite loop).

  • It doesn’t ask for new reasoning; it asks for reformat/repair only.

  • It keeps temperature 0 to reduce drift.

  • It can enforce required keys with null defaults.

Extra hardening (what I’d mention in interviews)

  • Reject JSON with extra keys (whitelist allowed keys).

  • Limit output size (avoid prompt injection causing huge output).

  • If using RAG/tools, don’t allow tool calls in “repair” step.

  • Log: prompt hash, raw output, parse errors, number of attempts.

[Image/Media]

Scenario – 4: Escalating Embedding Costs

Embedding expenses are rising rapidly. What caching or deduplication strategy would you design, and where would you store embeddings?


Answer:


Embedding cost grows because you keep re-embedding the same or near-same text:

  • same PDF re-uploaded

  • same chunks created again due to slightly different chunking

  • minor edits trigger full re-embed

  • multiple environments (staging/prod) embedding separately

So I’d solve it with dedup + caching at 3 levels and store embeddings in a place optimized for vector retrieval.

Strategy I’d design

1) Document-level dedup (skip entire file)

Goal: If the exact same document is uploaded again, do zero work.

  • Compute doc_hash = sha256(file_bytes)

  • If doc_hash already indexed → reuse the same document_id

When it helps: frequent reuploads, retries, environment redeploys.

2) Chunk-level dedup (skip exact duplicate chunks)

Goal: Even inside a doc, many chunks repeat (headers/footers/disclaimers), or chunking reruns.

  • Normalize chunk text (whitespace, hyphenation fixes, lowercasing optional)

  • Compute chunk_hash = sha256(normalized_chunk_text)

  • If chunk_hash already has an embedding → reuse it

Key point: chunk hash must include the embedding model version:

  • embed_key = f"{model_name}:{model_version}:{chunk_hash}"

So if you change embedding model later, you naturally re-embed once.

3) Near-duplicate detection (skip “almost same” chunks)

Exact hash won’t catch small edits. Add a cheap near-dup layer:

  • Create a SimHash (or MinHash) of the normalized text

  • Use it to find candidates that are “almost same”

  • If near-duplicate found above a threshold → reuse existing embedding

This is extremely useful when docs get minor edits (“v1.1” with 2 lines changed).

Where I’d store embeddings

Best practical architecture

Use two stores:

  1. Relational DB (Postgres) for cache + metadata

    • stores: doc hash, chunk hash, model version, text, chunk metadata, embedding id

    • fast lookups for dedup/caching

  2. Vector store for retrieval

    • If scale is small/medium: Postgres + pgvector (simple + one system)

    • If scale is large / high QPS: dedicated vector DB (Milvus, Pinecone, Weaviate, etc.)

Simple and effective choice: Postgres + pgvector

Because you can:

  • do dedup lookups + store vectors + filter by document/user/tenant in one DB

Data model (beginner-friendly)

Tables

documents

  • id

  • doc_hash (unique)

  • source_name, created_at

chunks

  • id

  • document_id

  • chunk_index

  • chunk_hash (unique per model)

  • text

  • metadata (page, section)

embeddings

  • id

  • embed_model

  • chunk_hash

  • vector (pgvector column)

  • unique constraint on (embed_model, chunk_hash)

Implementation (plain Python)

Normalization + hashing

import hashlib
import re

def normalize_text(text: str) -> str:
    text = text.replace("\\u00ad", "")                 # soft hyphen
    text = re.sub(r"-\\s+\\n", "", text)                # hyphen line breaks
    text = re.sub(r"\\s+", " ", text)                  # collapse spaces/newlines
    return text.strip()

def sha256_str(s: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(s.encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()

def sha256_bytes(b: bytes) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(b).hexdigest()

Cache key design

def embedding_cache_key(model_id: str, chunk_text: str) -> str:
    norm = normalize_text(chunk_text)
    chunk_hash = sha256_str(norm)
    return f"{model_id}:{chunk_hash}", chunk_hash, norm

Get-or-create embedding

(Uses a DB abstraction; in real code you’d implement db.get_embedding_by_key, db.insert_embedding, etc.)

def get_or_create_embedding(model_id: str, chunk_text: str, embed_fn, db):
    cache_key, chunk_hash, norm = embedding_cache_key(model_id, chunk_text)

    cached = db.get_embedding(model_id=model_id, chunk_hash=chunk_hash)
    if cached:
        return cached["vector"], chunk_hash

    vec = embed_fn(norm)  # call provider once
    db.insert_embedding(model_id=model_id, chunk_hash=chunk_hash, vector=vec, text=norm)
    return vec, chunk_hash

Document-level dedup

def ingest_document(file_bytes: bytes, chunks: list[str], model_id: str, embed_fn, db):
    doc_hash = sha256_bytes(file_bytes)

    existing_doc = db.get_document_by_hash(doc_hash)
    if existing_doc:
        return existing_doc["id"]  # already indexed

    doc_id = db.insert_document(doc_hash=doc_hash)

    for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        vec, chunk_hash = get_or_create_embedding(model_id, chunk, embed_fn, db)
        db.insert_chunk(
            document_id=doc_id,
            chunk_index=i,
            chunk_hash=chunk_hash,
            text=normalize_text(chunk),
        )
        db.upsert_vector_index(chunk_id=(doc_id, i), vector=vec, metadata={"doc_id": doc_id, "i": i})

    return doc_id

Extra cost controls I’d add

  • Batch embedding (embed 100 chunks per request if API supports it)

  • TTL cache in Redis for “hot” embeddings in real-time pipelines

  • Do not embed boilerplate (headers/footers): detect repeated lines and drop them

  • Incremental embedding: if doc updated, only embed changed chunks (hash diff)

Summary interview answer

I’d reduce embedding spend by hashing and caching at the document and chunk level, adding near-duplicate detection for minor edits, and storing embeddings keyed by (model_version + chunk_hash) so we reuse vectors safely across re-ingests. I’d store metadata and cache keys in Postgres, and store vectors in pgvector (or a dedicated vector DB at scale) so retrieval and filtering remain fast and consistent.

[Image/Media]

Scenario – 5: Custom Tool-Oriented Agent Design

You need tool-calling without LangChain (calculator, web search, vector search). How would you design the tool schema, argument parsing, and re-invocation loop?


Answer:


High-level design (plain Python, no LangChain)

I’d build a small “agent runtime” with 4 parts:

  1. Tool schema (machine-readable): name, description, JSON schema for args, and the Python function to run.

  2. Argument parsing + validation: parse tool call JSON, validate required fields + types, reject unknown args.

  3. Re-invocation loop: LLM → (maybe) tool call → run tool → append tool result → call LLM again until “final”.

  4. Safety + limits: max tool calls, timeouts, allowlist tools, strict output format.

1) Tool schema (simple and strict)

Each tool has:

  • name

  • description

  • parameters (JSON-schema-like)

  • handler(args) -> str (tool result as text or JSON string)

Example tools: calculator, web_search, vector_search.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any, Callable, Dict, List

@dataclass
class Tool:
    name: str
    description: str
    parameters: Dict[str, Any]         # JSON schema-ish
    handler: Callable[[Dict[str, Any]], Any]

TOOLS: List[Tool] = []
TOOL_REGISTRY: Dict[str, Tool] = {}

Example schemas

calculator_tool = Tool(
    name="calculator",
    description="Evaluate a basic arithmetic expression safely.",
    parameters={
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "expression": {"type": "string", "description": "e.g., '12*(5+3)'"}
        },
        "required": ["expression"],
        "additionalProperties": False,
    },
    handler=None,  # set later
)

web_search_tool = Tool(
    name="web_search",
    description="Search the web for recent information and return top results.",
    parameters={
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "query": {"type": "string"},
            "top_k": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 10},
        },
        "required": ["query"],
        "additionalProperties": False,
    },
    handler=None,
)

vector_search_tool = Tool(
    name="vector_search",
    description="Search an embedding index for relevant chunks.",
    parameters={
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "query": {"type": "string"},
            "top_k": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 20},
            "filter": {"type": "object"}  # optional metadata filters
        },
        "required": ["query"],
        "additionalProperties": False,
    },
    handler=None,
)

Register them:

def register_tools(tools: List[Tool]):
    for t in tools:
        TOOL_REGISTRY[t.name] = t

register_tools([calculator_tool, web_search_tool, vector_search_tool])

2) Argument parsing + validation (no “best effort”)

You want strict parsing because tool-calling is a boundary where errors and prompt injection happen.

Minimal validator (beginner-friendly, but effective)

class ToolValidationError(Exception):
    pass

def validate_args(schema: Dict[str, Any], args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    # additionalProperties = False means reject unknown keys
    if schema.get("additionalProperties") is False:
        allowed = set(schema.get("properties", {}).keys())
        unknown = set(args.keys()) - allowed
        if unknown:
            raise ToolValidationError(f"Unknown arguments: {sorted(unknown)}")

    # required keys
    for req in schema.get("required", []):
        if req not in args:
            raise ToolValidationError(f"Missing required argument: {req}")

    # type checks (basic)
    props = schema.get("properties", {})
    for k, v in args.items():
        expected = props.get(k, {}).get("type")
        if expected == "string" and not isinstance(v, str):
            raise ToolValidationError(f"Argument '{k}' must be string.")
        if expected == "integer" and not isinstance(v, int):
            raise ToolValidationError(f"Argument '{k}' must be integer.")
        if expected == "object" and not isinstance(v, dict):
            raise ToolValidationError(f"Argument '{k}' must be object (dict).")

        # min/max for integers
        if expected == "integer":
            mn = props.get(k, {}).get("minimum")
            mx = props.get(k, {}).get("maximum")
            if mn is not None and v < mn:
                raise ToolValidationError(f"Argument '{k}' must be >= {mn}.")
            if mx is not None and v > mx:
                raise ToolValidationError(f"Argument '{k}' must be <= {mx}.")

    return args

3) Tool call message format

I’d enforce a single, predictable structure from the LLM when it wants a tool:

{
  "type": "tool_call",
  "name": "vector_search",
  "arguments": { "query": "...", "top_k": 5 }
}

And when it’s done:

{
  "type": "final",
  "answer": "..."
}

This makes parsing trivial and avoids “hidden tool calls” in text.

4) Re-invocation loop (core agent runtime)

Key rules

  • max iterations (e.g., 5)

  • only allow tools from registry

  • tool errors get returned to the LLM as tool results (so it can correct args)

  • keep a structured “messages” list (system, user, assistant, tool)

import json
from typing import Optional

class ToolNotFoundError(Exception):
    pass

def run_tool(name: str, arguments: Dict[str, Any]) -> Any:
    tool = TOOL_REGISTRY.get(name)
    if not tool:
        raise ToolNotFoundError(f"Tool '{name}' not found.")

    validated = validate_args(tool.parameters, arguments)
    return tool.handler(validated)

def llm_call(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    """
    Placeholder: call your LLM here.
    Must return a JSON string representing either tool_call or final.
    """
    raise NotImplementedError

def agent_loop(user_input: str, max_steps: int = 5) -> str:
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": (
            "You are a tool-using assistant.\\n"
            "When needed, respond ONLY with JSON in one of these forms:\\n"
            "1) {\\"type\\":\\"tool_call\\",\\"name\\":\\"...\\",\\"arguments\\":{...}}\\n"
            "2) {\\"type\\":\\"final\\",\\"answer\\":\\"...\\"}\\n"
        )},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_input},
    ]

    for step in range(1, max_steps + 1):
        raw = llm_call(messages)

        # Strict JSON parse
        try:
            obj = json.loads(raw)
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            # Tell model to fix formatting (no tool call)
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw})
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": "Return valid JSON only."})
            continue

        msg_type = obj.get("type")
        if msg_type == "final":
            return obj.get("answer", "")

        if msg_type != "tool_call":
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": "Invalid response type. Use tool_call or final."})
            continue

        tool_name = obj.get("name")
        tool_args = obj.get("arguments", {})

        # Run tool safely
        try:
            tool_result = run_tool(tool_name, tool_args)
            tool_payload = json.dumps(tool_result, ensure_ascii=False) if not isinstance(tool_result, str) else tool_result
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw})  # tool call record
            messages.append({"role": "tool", "name": tool_name, "content": tool_payload})
        except (ToolValidationError, ToolNotFoundError) as e:
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw})
            messages.append({"role": "tool", "name": tool_name or "unknown", "content": f"ERROR: {str(e)}"})
        except Exception as e:
            # unexpected tool failure
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": raw})
            messages.append({"role": "tool", "name": tool_name or "unknown", "content": f"TOOL_CRASH: {repr(e)}"})

    return "I couldn't complete the task within tool-call limits."

5) Example tool handlers (calculator + vector search)

Safe calculator (no eval)

import ast
import operator as op

_ALLOWED = {
    ast.Add: op.add, ast.Sub: op.sub, ast.Mult: op.mul, ast.Div: op.truediv,
    ast.FloorDiv: op.floordiv, ast.Mod: op.mod, ast.Pow: op.pow,
    ast.USub: op.neg, ast.UAdd: op.pos,
}

def safe_eval(expr: str) -> float:
    def _eval(node):
        if isinstance(node, ast.Num):  # py<3.8
            return node.n
        if isinstance(node, ast.Constant):  # py>=3.8
            if isinstance(node.value, (int, float)):
                return node.value
            raise ValueError("Only numbers allowed.")
        if isinstance(node, ast.BinOp):
            return _ALLOWED[type(node.op)](_eval(node.left), _eval(node.right))
        if isinstance(node, ast.UnaryOp):
            return _ALLOWED[type(node.op)](_eval(node.operand))
        raise ValueError("Unsupported expression.")

    tree = ast.parse(expr, mode="eval")
    return _eval(tree.body)

def calculator_handler(args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    value = safe_eval(args["expression"])
    return {"result": value}

TOOL_REGISTRY["calculator"].handler = calculator_handler

Vector search handler (FAISS-style interface)

Assume you have retriever.search(query, top_k) returning chunks.

def vector_search_handler(args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    query = args["query"]
    top_k = args.get("top_k", 5)
    flt = args.get("filter", {})

    results = retriever.search(query=query, top_k=top_k, metadata_filter=flt)
    # results -> [{"text": "...", "score": 0.82, "meta": {...}}, ...]
    return {"matches": results}

TOOL_REGISTRY["vector_search"].handler = vector_search_handler

6) What I’d highlight in an interview (practical points)

  • Strict JSON interface for tool calls prevents messy parsing.

  • Schema validation blocks malformed args and reduces tool abuse.

  • Deterministic loop with max_steps avoids infinite tool chains.

  • Tool results go back as messages so the LLM can reason with them.

  • Security: allowlist tools, safe calculator, timeouts for web search, sanitize outputs, and never execute code from the model.

[Image/Media]

Scenario – 1: Retrieval Quality Degradation

You built a RAG app using LangChain’s RetrievalQA. Users report irrelevant answers. How would you debug retriever configuration, chunking strategy, and prompt template?


Answer:

1) Reproduce + “open the box” first (most important)

When users say “irrelevant answers,” the first job is to see what the chain actually retrieved and fed to the LLM.

In LangChain, enable returning source docs (or run retriever directly) so you can inspect:

  • the top-k chunks

  • their metadata (page, section)

  • their similarity scores (if available)

  • the final prompt that went to the LLM (if you have tracing)

RetrievalQA supports returning source documents via return_source_documents=True in the QA-with-sources style and similar patterns.


Minimal debug pattern

# 1) Call retriever directly
docs = retriever.get_relevant_documents(question)
print("Retrieved:", len(docs))
for i, d in enumerate(docs[:5], 1):
    print(f"\\n#{i} meta={d.metadata}")
    print(d.page_content[:700])

# 2) Then call the chain
result = qa_chain({"query": question})
print("\\nANSWER:", result["result"])
print("\\nSOURCES:", [d.metadata for d in result.get("source_documents", [])])

If retrieved chunks are irrelevant → retriever/chunking issue.

If retrieved chunks are good but answer is still off → prompt / chain_type issue.

2) Debug retriever configuration (k, search_type, thresholds, filters)

Common misconfigs that cause irrelevant retrieval

  1. k too high: you stuff too many weak chunks and the model gets distracted.

  2. k too low: you miss the key chunk and the model guesses.

  3. Wrong search_type:

    • similarity can return near-duplicate chunks (low diversity)

    • mmr improves diversity (often helps relevance)

  4. Bad score_threshold:

    • too low → lots of garbage

    • too high → nothing returned (model hallucinates)

  5. Missing / incorrect metadata filter (mixing multiple PDFs/tenants)

Concrete fixes to try (in order)

A) Print what your retriever returns with scores

Some vectorstores expose similarity_search_with_score().

B) Tune retriever search kwargs

retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(
    search_type="mmr",  # try "similarity" vs "mmr"
    search_kwargs={
        "k": 4,
        "fetch_k": 20,       # candidate pool for MMR
        "lambda_mult": 0.5,  # diversity tradeoff
        # "score_threshold": 0.7,  # only if using similarity_score_threshold
        # "filter": {"doc_id": "policy.pdf"}  # if you have metadata
    },
)

LangChain retrievers commonly support search_type like similarity, mmr, or similarity_score_threshold, and kwargs like k, fetch_k, lambda_mult, and score_threshold.

C) If users ask short/vague queries

Add query rewriting (multi-query retriever) or prepend a domain hint. Even a simple rule helps:

  • “In the context of this PDF, answer: …”

3) Debug chunking strategy (chunk_size, overlap, separators, metadata)

Chunking failures that look like “irrelevant answers”

  • chunks too big → mixed topics, embedding becomes “blurry”

  • chunks too small → missing context, definitions split

  • no overlap → answer spans boundary → retrieval misses

  • poor PDF extraction (headers/footers repeated, broken lines)

What I would measure

  • Avg chunk length (tokens or chars)

  • % of chunks that are repeated boilerplate (header/footer)

  • For 10 failing questions: does the correct chunk exist, and what rank does it appear?

Practical baseline for PDFs

Use a “natural boundary” splitter (paragraph → sentence → word) with overlap.

Many tutorials and examples use something like RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with ~1000 chars chunk size and ~200 overlap as a starting point (then tune).

Fixes

  • Add overlap (100–200 chars)

  • Strip repeated headers/footers before splitting

  • Keep metadata: page, section_title, chunk_id

  • If document has headings, consider “section-based chunking” (split by headings first)

4) Debug the prompt template + chain type (stuff vs map_reduce vs refine)

Why prompt/template causes irrelevance

Even with good retrieval, the LLM can:

  • ignore context

  • answer from prior knowledge

  • merge unrelated chunks into a plausible-sounding answer

Fix the prompt template (must be strict)

A good RetrievalQA prompt should:

  • say use only provided context

  • allow “I don’t know from the docs”

  • ask for citations (forces grounding)

Example template:

from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate

prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["context", "question"],
    template=(
        "You are answering using ONLY the context below.\\n"
        "If the answer is not in the context, say: \\"I don't know based on the document.\\"\\n\\n"
        "Context:\\n{context}\\n\\n"
        "Question: {question}\\n\\n"
        "Answer (concise):"
    ),
)

Choose the right chain_type

  • stuff: fastest, but can become noisy if many chunks are stuffed

  • map_reduce: summarize per doc/chunk then combine; helps when lots of context

  • refine: iteratively improves answer with each chunk; can help for long docs but may drift if early chunk is wrong

If irrelevance comes from “too much context,” switching from stuff to map_reduce or reducing k often helps.

5) A tight debugging checklist (what I’d say in an interview)

  1. Turn on source_documents / inspect retrieved docs.

  2. If retrieval is bad:

    • tune k, try mmr, add thresholds/filters

    • fix chunking: size/overlap, remove boilerplate, preserve structure

  3. If retrieval is good but answer is still wrong:

    • tighten prompt: “only use context”, “I don’t know”

    • require citations / evidence lines

    • try map_reduce or refine instead of stuff

  4. Add a small eval set (20–50 Qs) and track:

    • “correct chunk in top-k?”

    • “answer grounded in retrieved chunk?”

[Image/Media]

Scenario – 2: Incorrect Tool Selection

Your LangChain agent using tools sometimes selects the wrong tool. How would you improve tool descriptions and better control agent reasoning?


Answer:

1) Why agents pick the wrong tool

Common reasons:

  • Tool descriptions are vague/overlapping (“search” vs “vector_search” both sound similar)

  • No clear “when to use / when NOT to use”

  • Missing argument examples, so the agent guesses

  • Agent is allowed to answer without tools, so it “shortcuts”

  • Too many tools with similar names

  • Prompt doesn’t force a “tool selection policy”

2) Improve tool descriptions (make them decision-friendly)

A good tool description should include Purpose + When to use + When not to use + Inputs + Output + Example.

Before (bad)

“search: searches for information”

After (good)

web_search

  • Use when: user asks for latest, current, news, pricing, today, or anything that might have changed recently.

  • Do NOT use when: answer is present in the uploaded PDF / vector index.

  • Input: query string

  • Output: list of web results (title, snippet, url)

vector_search

  • Use when: question is about the uploaded/internal documents.

  • Do NOT use when: user asks for “today/latest” beyond document scope.

  • Input: query, optional filter like doc_id

  • Output: top chunks + scores + metadata

calculator

  • Use when: arithmetic / numeric evaluation is needed.

  • Do NOT use when: user wants explanation only.

This “when / when-not” reduces confusion drastically.

3) Add a tool selection policy in the system prompt

Put a small, strict decision tree in the system prompt (agents follow this surprisingly well):

Tool Selection Rules (must follow):
1) If the user asks for math calculation, use calculator.
2) If the user asks about uploaded docs, policies, PDFs, or "according to the document", use vector_search.
3) If the user asks for current events, latest info, prices, or anything time-sensitive, use web_search.
4) If unsure between web_search and vector_search:
   - If question contains "in the document" or doc-specific terms -> vector_search
   - Else -> web_search
Do not answer from memory when a tool is required.

Also: rename tools to remove ambiguity:

  • search → web_search

  • retrieve → doc_search / vector_search

4) Reduce overlap and add “negative examples” per tool

In the tool description itself, include examples of wrong usage:

vector_search

  • ✅ “What does the onboarding PDF say about leave policy?”

  • ❌ “What is the current CEO of OpenAI?”

web_search

  • ✅ “What’s the latest Node.js LTS version?”

  • ❌ “Explain chapter 3 of the uploaded handbook.”

Agents learn from these contrasts.

5) Control agent reasoning better (practical methods)

A) Force “plan → tool → answer” structure (without showing chain-of-thought)

You don’t need to reveal reasoning, but you can require a short structured “decision”:

Before calling any tool, output:
DECISION: <tool_name> | NONE
WHY: <one short sentence>
ARGS: <json>

Then execute the tool.

This makes tool choice explicit and auditable.

B) Use structured tool calling (function calling) instead of free-text

If you let the agent output tool calls as plain text, it will drift.

Use function calling / structured tools so the model must pick:

  • tool name from allowed list

  • args matching schema

This reduces “wrong tool due to formatting” and makes routing consistent.

C) Add a “router” step (two-model or two-pass)

Make tool selection a separate step:

  1. Router prompt: choose tool only (no answering).

  2. Run tool.

  3. Answer prompt: answer using tool output.

This reduces tool confusion because step-1 has a single job.

Router output schema:

{"tool":"vector_search","arguments":{"query":"...","top_k":5}}

D) Add tool-choice tests and feedback loop

Create 30–50 typical queries and label the correct tool.

Run daily tests: “tool accuracy %”.

If tool accuracy drops, you know prompt/tool descriptions regressed.

6) Concrete “improved tool spec” example

tools = [
  {
    "name": "vector_search",
    "description": (
      "Search the uploaded/internal documents for answers.\\n"
      "Use when the question refers to the PDF/docs, policies, or anything 'according to the document'.\\n"
      "Do NOT use for current events, prices, latest versions.\\n"
      "Returns relevant text chunks with scores and metadata.\\n"
      "Example: 'What does the handbook say about parental leave?'"
    ),
    "parameters": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "query": {"type": "string"},
        "top_k": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 20},
        "filter": {"type": "object"}
      },
      "required": ["query"],
      "additionalProperties": False
    }
  },
  {
    "name": "web_search",
    "description": (
      "Search the public web for up-to-date info.\\n"
      "Use for latest/news/pricing/current versions.\\n"
      "Do NOT use if the answer should come from uploaded documents.\\n"
      "Example: 'What is the latest React version?'"
    ),
    "parameters": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "query": {"type": "string"},
        "top_k": {"type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 10}
      },
      "required": ["query"],
      "additionalProperties": False
    }
  }
]

7) Summary actions I’d take

  • Rewrite tool descriptions with when / when-not / examples

  • Reduce overlap + rename tools clearly

  • Add a tool selection policy to system prompt

  • Enforce structured function calling with strict schemas

  • Optionally add a router step for tool selection

  • Add a small tool-choice eval set to prevent regressions

[Image/Media]

Scenario – 3: Memory Saturation Issue

You are using ConversationBufferMemory. After long chats, responses degrade. Why does this happen and which memory strategy would you switch to?


Answer:

Why responses degrade with ConversationBufferMemory

ConversationBufferMemory keeps appending the entire chat history and sends it back to the LLM each time. After long chats this causes:

  1. Context window pressure

  • LLMs have a fixed context limit (tokens).

  • When history grows, either:

    • you hit the limit and the system truncates older messages, or

    • you must trim manually, which often removes important info.

      Result: the model “forgets” key details or keeps only recent, possibly irrelevant parts.

  1. Noise increases

  • Old small-talk, repeated clarifications, and irrelevant turns remain in the prompt.

  • The model has to “search” through more text to find what matters.

    Result: it latches onto wrong details, contradicts itself, or becomes generic.

  1. Cost + latency go up

  • More tokens per request = slower + more expensive.

    This indirectly causes production issues like timeouts/retries that further degrade quality.

Which memory strategy I’d switch to (and why)

Option A) ConversationSummaryMemory (best general replacement)

Instead of keeping the full history, it keeps a running summary of older messages and preserves recent turns.

Why it helps

  • Stable prompt size

  • Keeps key decisions/preferences/goals

  • Reduces noise

When to use

  • Long support chats

  • Coaching / tutoring sessions

  • Anything where “what we agreed earlier” matters

Option B) ConversationSummaryBufferMemory (often the best practical choice)

This combines:

  • a moving window of the most recent messages (verbatim)

  • a summary of older messages

Why it’s strong

  • Recent details stay exact

  • Older context is compressed

  • Works well for long ongoing conversations

Option C) ConversationBufferWindowMemory (quick/simple fix)

Keep only the last N turns (like last 10 messages).

Pros

  • Very simple

  • Fast and predictable

Cons

  • Loses older important facts completely

  • Not good if users refer to earlier commitments

Option D) Vector-based / retrieval memory (best when history is huge)

Store conversation snippets as embeddings and retrieve only relevant past parts per new query.

When to use

  • Very long chats (hundreds/thousands of turns)

  • “Remember what I said earlier about X” style conversations

Tradeoff

  • More engineering complexity

  • Needs good retrieval + dedup

My recommended switch (interview-ready)

If quality degrades after long chats, I’d switch from ConversationBufferMemory to ConversationSummaryBufferMemory so the system keeps a compact summary of older context plus the latest turns verbatim. This prevents context overflow, reduces noise, and keeps responses consistent without losing important long-term information.

[Image/Media]

Scenario – 4: Structured Output Instability

Your StructuredOutputParser frequently fails due to formatting errors. How would you make output parsing more reliable?


Answer:

Why StructuredOutputParser fails so often

Most failures come from the model producing:

  • extra text (“Sure, here you go…”)

  • markdown fences (```json)

  • missing quotes / single quotes

  • trailing commas

  • wrong key names / missing required fields

  • wrong types (string instead of list, etc.)

  • partial JSON due to token limits

So the fix is: tighten the generation contract + validate + repair with bounded retries, and where possible use schema-enforced generation.

Make parsing reliable: practical approach

1) Prefer schema-enforced output (best fix)

If your LLM/provider supports structured outputs / JSON schema mode / function calling, use it so the model must return JSON that matches a schema.

This immediately removes most formatting errors.

2) Strengthen the prompt contract (even if using StructuredOutputParser)

Use a prompt that:

  • explicitly forbids markdown and extra text

  • includes the schema (or format instructions)

  • allows “null” for unknown fields instead of inventing keys

  • provides a small example

Example (conceptual):

  • “Return ONLY a JSON object.”

  • “No markdown.”

  • “Use double quotes.”

  • “If a field is unknown, set it to null.”

3) Reduce degrees of freedom (simplify the schema)

The more complex your schema, the more failures:

  • deep nesting

  • unions like “string OR list”

  • optional fields with unclear rules

Make it easier:

  • keep shallow objects

  • fixed keys

  • predictable types

  • avoid free-form text arrays if possible

4) Add strict validation and a repair loop (most important in production)

Do not “assume” the parser will work. Implement:

  1. LLM call → parse

  2. if parse fails → send a repair prompt with:

    • the invalid output

    • the exact schema

    • instruction: “Return only corrected JSON. Don’t change meaning.”

  3. max retries (2–3)

Plain Python repair loop (generic)

import json
import re

def extract_json_object(text: str) -> str | None:
    text = text.strip()
    # Try direct JSON first
    try:
        json.loads(text)
        return text
    except Exception:
        pass
    # Extract first {...} block
    m = re.search(r"\\{.*\\}", text, flags=re.DOTALL)
    return m.group(0).strip() if m else None

def parse_or_repair(llm_call, base_messages, schema_instructions: str, max_attempts: int = 3):
    last_raw = ""
    for attempt in range(1, max_attempts + 1):
        raw = llm_call(base_messages)
        last_raw = raw

        candidate = extract_json_object(raw)
        if candidate:
            try:
                return json.loads(candidate)
            except Exception:
                pass

        # Repair attempt message (bounded)
        base_messages = base_messages + [{
            "role": "user",
            "content": (
                "Your output is invalid.\\n"
                "Fix ONLY the formatting to match the schema exactly.\\n"
                "Return ONLY a JSON object. No extra text. No markdown.\\n\\n"
                f"SCHEMA:\\n{schema_instructions}\\n\\n"
                f"INVALID OUTPUT:\\n{last_raw}"
            )
        }]

    raise ValueError(f"Failed to parse after {max_attempts} attempts. Last output: {last_raw[:400]}")

Why this works

  • Most “failures” are cosmetic formatting issues.

  • The repair prompt focuses the model on conversion, not new content.

  • Bounded retries prevent infinite loops.

5) Add output “self-check” before returning

Have the model do a short internal validation step in the same call (without showing it) by requiring it to output only JSON after verifying it parses.

Prompt rule example:

  • “Before responding, verify your JSON parses. If not, fix it silently.”

This doesn’t guarantee correctness, but it reduces errors.

6) Handle truncation and token limits

If outputs are often cut off:

  • reduce verbosity (ask for shorter fields)

  • increase max_tokens

  • split tasks (first produce key fields, then optional fields)

  • avoid large arrays in one response

7) Add guardrails for common failures

In validation code:

  • reject unknown keys (prevents hallucinated fields)
